title: AI Threat Intel Inside National Policy
modules: Why Siloed Threat Data Became a National Security Liability
How AI Accelerates Threat Intelligence Analysis and Sharing
Legal and Sovereignty Constraints on Cross-Border AI Intel
Frameworks in Practice: CISA, MITRE ATT&CK, and EU AI Act Intersections
Information Sharing Agreements: What AI Can and Cannot Automate
Case Study: State-Sponsored AI Campaigns and the Policy Response Gap
Designing Compliant AI-Augmented Threat Sharing Programs
synopsis: Nation-state actors are using AI to accelerate cyberattacks while defenders remain hamstrung by legal, political, and technical barriers to sharing the threat intelligence that could stop them. This course maps the policy landscape, shows where AI can legally and practically accelerate sharing, and prepares professionals to operate inside real compliance constraints.
rationale: As governments race to legislate AI use in critical infrastructure, security professionals have almost no structured guidance on where AI-augmented threat sharing is legally permissible and operationally effective.
learning_outcome: Learners can assess their organization's threat intelligence sharing posture against current national policy frameworks and identify specific AI-augmented sharing workflows that remain legally compliant.
source_signals: Trump AI plan calls for cybersecurity assessments, threat info-sharing
Microsoft Releases 2025 Digital Defense Report: Highlighting the Changing Cyber Threat Landscape and the Importance of Security in the AI Era
